- [ ] Watchdog reaps workers past `WatchdogDeadline` with no phase transition in `StallDeadline`
- [ ] SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation works on an intentionally-wedged test worker (unit test with a goroutine that ignores ctx)

- [ ] Watchdog deadlines are configurable via ddx server config","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T05:48:25Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"13cef95535aea82c1f00dd596704f141ed131af7","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:43:00.09109294Z","description":"## Residual risk after RC1–RC4 from ddx-0a651925

Even with context propagation (RC1), wall-clock bounds (RC2), tightened breakers (RC3), and provider deadlines (RC4), a goroutine-level deadlock or panic-swallowed handler could still leave a worker alive past any reasonable bound. Today, `server.WorkerManager.Stop` (workers.go:648) only calls `handle.cancel()` — it has no escalation to OS-level signal and no autonomous trigger. ddx-b808df39 adds the *operator* tool; this bead adds the *autonomous* watchdog.

## Scope

1. WorkerManager runs a supervisor goroutine that, every minute, inspects each `workerHandle`.
2. If `time.Since(record.StartedAt) > WatchdogDeadline` (default e.g. 6h) AND `record.CurrentAttempt` has not transitioned in `StallDeadline` (default e.g. 1h), escalate:
   - Send SIGTERM to the worker's process group (requires surfacing PID, aligned with ddx-b808df39).
   - After 30s grace, send SIGKILL.
   - Mark record.State = "reaped"; update claimed bead state so the claim is not leaked.
3. Emit a `bead.reaped` event on the tracker with the full timeline so post-mortems are easy.

## Non-goals

- Per-bead runtime tuning; defaults are enough.
- Replacing ddx-b808df39 — this is defense-in-depth, not duplication. ddx-b808df39 is operator-initiated; this bead is system-initiated.

## Dependencies

- ddx-b808df39 surfaces PID in `ddx agent workers --json` output; this bead reuses that plumbing.","events_attachment":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-34d039b7/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-18T06:02:55.327228469Z","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-34d039b7","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:enhancement","area:agent","area:workers","root-cause:defense-in-depth"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-0a651925","priority":1,"session_id":"eb-6ca39274","status":"closed","title":"execute-bead: autonomous worker watchdog with process-level reaper","updated_at":"2026-04-18T06:03:25.675050303Z"}

Provider call returns within deadline with a distinct error","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T05:30:50Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"b0a4fce4796e6841c9a9686ebeee197fb22a2fa7","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:42:38.821147268Z","description":"## Root cause RC4 from ddx-0a651925

After RC1 is fixed (caller ctx propagates), provider HTTP calls still need per-request deadlines. A stalled TCP socket that delivers headers and then stops sending body bytes — particularly during streaming token reads — can hang a goroutine for days.

Grepping the `DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14` module cache shows short timeouts only on discovery probes (`provider/openai/discovery.go:46,174`, `openai.go:123`). The main `Run` call path inside agent providers appears to inherit whatever ctx DDx passes — which today is `context.Background()` (see RC1).

## Scope

1. Audit `openai`, `anthropic`, `virtual` providers in the agent library for per-request deadline usage.
2. Install a per-request deadline (default ~10-15 min) on each provider call; separate from the wall-clock bound in RC2.
3. For streaming bodies, enforce an **idle-read limit** on the response body so a mid-stream stall cannot hold a goroutine indefinitely (e.g. `http.Transport.ResponseHeaderTimeout` + periodic `SetReadDeadline` on the underlying conn).
4. Surface provider-timeout failures as a distinct status in `ExecuteBeadResult.Detail`.

## Non-goals

- Rewriting providers; minimally invasive change only.
- Changing DDx-side timeout defaults (RC2 covers outer bound).

## Prerequisite

Sibling RC1 bead must be resolved first, otherwise adding deadlines inside providers is useless while DDx still feeds them `context.Background()`.","events_attachment":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-3e3913a0/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-18T05:47:50.145743886Z","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-3e3913a0","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:bug","area:agent","area:providers","root-cause:rc4"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-0a651925","priority":1,"session_id":"eb-b67b87ce","status":"closed","title":"execute-bead: audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent provider calls","updated_at":"2026-04-18T05:48:25.052955138Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:3:{"acceptance":"- [ ] `RunOptions` accepts a caller ctx, or `Runner.Run`/`Runner.RunAgent` accept ctx explicitly
- [ ] The internal cancel is derived from the caller ctx (no `context.Background()` seeds inside Run/RunAgent)
- [ ] `agent.ExecuteBead` takes ctx and passes it down
- [ ] `server.WorkerManager.Stop(id)` cancels the running agent provider call within 2s in a unit test (fake provider blocks on ctx)
- [ ] Regression test: runner with a canceled ctx returns promptly (<500ms) regardless of mocked provider latency","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T04:51:41Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"9b044bcebc981533d2d48d4ffd79a91743dee884","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:41:24.464808955Z","description":"## Root cause RC1 from ddx-0a651925

`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (Runner.Run) and `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (Runner.RunAgent) both call `context.WithCancel(context.Background())`, discarding any context from the caller. `RunOptions` exposes no ctx field. `agent.ExecuteBead` likewise takes no ctx, so `server.WorkerManager.runWorker`'s ctx never reaches the agent call.

As a result, `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (server/workers.go:648) cancels only the worker goroutine; the embedded `agentlib.Run` call (and any subprocess it launched) keeps running until SIGKILL. This is the structural defect behind 33–142h hung workers.

## Scope

1. Add `Context context.Context` to `RunOptions` (or change signatures to `Run(ctx, opts)`). Default to `context.Background()` when nil.
2. Derive the internal ctx from the caller's rather than `context.Background()`.
3. Thread ctx through `agent.ExecuteBead` → `runner.Run` → `agentlib.Run` → provider HTTP.
4. Update `server.WorkerManager.runWorker`'s `singleTierAttempt` closure to pass `ctx` into `agent.ExecuteBead`.

## Non-goals

- New wall-clock timeout logic (covered by sibling RC2 bead)
- Operator-facing stop command (covered by ddx-b808df39)

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:4:{"acceptance":"- [ ] Compaction-stuck detection fires within 15 minutes when no-op compaction events are the only activity, regardless of event rate
- [ ] No regression on the existing behaviour for healthy agents (normal runs do not trip)
- [ ] Unit test: fake agentlib emits one no-op compaction event every 3 seconds → breaker fires within 15 min
- [ ] Result.json `detail` clearly reports the time-based breaker when it fires","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-19T22:40:19Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"721226","closing_commit_sha":"7c69dc3ec37cdc89ddbaf2ce961001d451a32304","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:41:53.916108666Z","description":"## Root cause RC3 from ddx-0a651925

`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:218-241` trips at `stuckThreshold = maxConsecutiveCompactionFailures` (50 by default). Observed on bundle `20260416T230316-efbf91c5`: the breaker took **2h 8m** to fire (7,720,908 ms). If each iteration takes ~3 min to emit a no-op compaction event, 50 events = ~2.5h. The counter is useful but fires far too late to be a hang-guard.

## Scope

Either:
1. **Time-bounded** — fire after N no-op events **within M minutes** (e.g. 10 within 15m), OR
2. **Threshold lowered** — drop default from 50 to ~10 (only for agent harness where one iteration is already expensive), OR
3. **Wall-clock guard layered on top** — independent breaker fires after e.g. 20 min of consecutive no-op compaction events regardless of count.

Recommend option 1 or 3: time-based thresholds are more robust to provider-speed variance.

## Non-goals

- Changing the compaction algorithm itself.
- Extending breakers outside the compaction path.","events_attachment":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-b0562380/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-19T22:56:20.038156421Z","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-b0562380","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:enhancement","area:agent","root-cause:rc3"],"owner":"ddx","parent":"ddx-0a651925","priority":2,"session_id":"eb-e18e2206","status":"closed","title":"execute-bead: bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock time","updated_at":"2026-04-19T22:56:41.167757535Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:5:{"acceptance":"- [ ] New default wall-clock deadline (e.g. 3h) applied to both `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` and `Runner.RunAgent`
- [ ] Wall-clock timeout fires even when stdout/stderr/agent events are flowing (unit test: fake provider emits 1 event/sec for > deadline, expect cancel at deadline ± 1s)
- [ ] Idle timeout behaviour preserved (unit test: no activity for idle timeout → fire)
- [ ] Result.json distinguishes `timeout: wall-clock` from `timeout: idle`
- [ ] Documented in types.go (DefaultWallClockMS constant with comment)","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T05:15:25Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"fff2c86240b98cd687a9dc9879926b17411db3f5","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:41:40.15942901Z","description":"## Root cause RC2 from ddx-0a651925

The `DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000` (2h) in `cli/internal/agent/types.go:363` is advertised as a hard cap but implemented as an *idle timeout*:

- `cli/internal/agent/executor.go:154-178` resets `time.NewTimer(idleTimeout)` on every stdout/stderr byte.
- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:264-285` resets on every non-compaction agentlib event.

A provider that emits any heartbeat, retry line, or periodic event defeats the timer indefinitely — enabling the 33–142h hangs observed on agent projects.

Only the claude streaming path (`cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:373-384`) uses a true wall-clock deadline via `time.After(timeout)`. That path is why bundle `20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba` fired at exactly 2h 0m 0.098s — and nothing else does.

## Scope

1. Add an absolute wall-clock deadline in addition to (not replacing) the idle timeout.
2. Default the wall-clock bound to something operators can trust — e.g. 3x `DefaultTimeoutMS` or a new `DefaultWallClockMS`.
3. Apply in: `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` (subprocess path) and `Runner.RunAgent` (embedded agent path).
4. Reflect the wall-clock error in result.json as a distinct `error: "wall-clock deadline exceeded after Xh"` so it is not confused with idle timeout.

## Non-goals

- Lowering or removing the existing idle timeout.
- Per-provider tuning (can be follow-up).","events_attachment":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-b203c889/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-18T05:29:25.624987414Z","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-b203c889","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:bug","area:agent","area:workers","root-cause:rc2"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-0a651925","priority":0,"session_id":"eb-0acc00fd","status":"closed","title":"execute-bead: add wall-clock deadline alongside idle timeout for agent harness","updated_at":"2026-04-18T05:30:49.793036993Z"}

Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / response_format=json_object / tool-use-required. If a bead prompt depends on structured output, today routing's blindness means the failure surfaces at dispatch.

Per the parent bead's analysis, this is probably covered incidentally by the tool-calling gating sub-bead — most reasoning-tool-capable models also support structured output. This bead exists to either (a) explicitly add a SupportsStructuredOutput capability, or (b) close as 'covered by tool-calling gating' once that bead lands and we verify the assumption.

## Decision gate (do this first)

Before writing code, verify the 'covered by tool-calling' hypothesis:
- Survey the providers we currently route to (anthropic, openai, openrouter, omlx, lmstudio, ollama) and check whether each one's tool-calling support implies JSON-mode support.
- If yes everywhere we care about: close this bead with a note in the tool-calling bead's tests / docs that asserts the implication.
- If no (any counter-example): proceed with scope below.

## Scope (only if decision gate fails)

- Add SupportsStructuredOutput tri-state to ModelEntry / Surface and to capabilities.go's curated table.
- RouteRequest.RequiresStructuredOutput bool; execute-bead sets it when the prompt requires JSON mode.
- evaluateCandidate rejects RequiresStructuredOutput=true on SupportsStructuredOutput=no. Unknown passes with -v warning.

## Files (only if decision gate fails)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-22cadb21","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing"],"notes":"Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing","parent":"ddx-4817edfd","priority":4,"status":"closed","title":"routing: structured-output (JSON-mode) gating — likely incidental to tool-calling","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:43:47.191941677Z"}

Some omlx flavors and older local OpenAI-compat servers do not implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Today routing fuzzy-matches them and dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' — or worse, silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop cannot consume.

## Scope

- Per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata: SupportsTools bool. Two layers:
  1. Hand-curated table for known harnesses (claude, codex, opus, etc.) and provider flavors (openai, anthropic, openrouter, omlx, lmstudio, ollama).
  2. Discovery-driven enrichment when /v1/models/status (or equivalent) advertises tool support per model.
- RouteRequest gains RequiresTools bool (default false to preserve current behavior; execute-bead sets true).
- evaluateCandidate rejects candidates where RequiresTools=true and the resolved (provider, flavor, model) combo is known NOT to support tools. Unknown = pass with -v warning, same pattern as context-window bead.
- Capability metadata location: cli/internal/agent/catalog.go on ModelEntry/Surface, plus a curated table in cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (or co-located).

## Out of scope

- Context-window, structured-output, effort, permissions gating (sibling beads).
- Probing servers actively at routing time (uses cached discovery + table).

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/types.go (RouteRequest.RequiresTools, capability fields on CandidatePlan)
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go (rejection in rejectIfNotViable / evaluateCandidate)
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go (SupportsTools on ModelEntry / Surface)
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (NEW — curated table)","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-854cb8a7","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing","area:catalog"],"notes":"Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing","parent":"ddx-4817edfd","priority":2,"status":"closed","title":"routing: tool-calling support gating with per-flavor capability metadata","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:43:47.285105492Z"}

2. evaluateCandidate rejects candidates that don't honor a RouteRequest's --effort or --permissions, with a debug reason that names which capability and which provider/model.
3. Unknown capability does NOT reject; logs at -v.
4. Unit tests cover: known-no-effort rejection, known-yes pass, unknown pass with warning, no-effort-requested disables the check (parallel structure for permissions).
5. go test ./cli/... is green.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"5328120be55ab6af07a5cd35c7dc7f7a22f30f96","created_at":"2026-04-18T06:50:41.86041297Z","description":"## Provider-specific honoring of --effort and --permissions (sibling of ddx-4817edfd).

Today, Harness.EffortFlag is checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable, so harnesses without effort support are rejected when --effort is set. But provider-level gating is missing: e.g., openrouter doesn't honor --effort on non-reasoning models, and --permissions unrestricted may not apply to every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and silently no-op the flag.

## Scope

- Add effort/permissions capability fields to the curated capability table (same file added by the tool-calling sub-bead, or co-locate here if that one ships first).
  - SupportsEffort tri-state per (provider, model).
  - SupportsPermission(permission_class) — at minimum 'unrestricted'.
- evaluateCandidate / rejectIfNotViable: when RouteRequest names an --effort or --permissions value not honored by the resolved (provider, model) combo, reject with a clear reason. Unknown = pass with -v warning.
- Document expected behavior for each known provider in capabilities.go comments (openrouter non-reasoning models = no effort, etc.).

## Out of scope

- Context-window, tool-calling, structured-output gating (sibling beads).
- Building a 'downgrade' path (e.g., silently strip --effort and continue). Rejection is sufficient for v1; downgrade is a follow-up.

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (shared with tool-calling bead — or new file if order differs)
- cli/internal/agent/types.go","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-bb230ad5","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing"],"notes":"Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing","parent":"ddx-4817edfd","priority":3,"status":"closed","title":"routing: effort/permissions gating per provider+model (not just per harness)","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:43:47.3806568Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:21:{"acceptance":"1. RouteRequest has EstimatedPromptTokens uint32 field, zero value means 'unspecified'.
2. evaluateCandidate rejects candidates whose known context window < EstimatedPromptTokens; emits a routing-debug reason that names the model and its window.
3. Unknown context window does NOT cause rejection; logs a warning at -v.
4. execute-bead populates EstimatedPromptTokens with at least a char/4 estimate of the assembled prompt.
5. Unit test in routing_test.go covers: (a) below-window rejection, (b) above-window pass, (c) unknown-window pass with warning, (d) EstimatedPromptTokens=0 disables the check.
6. go test ./cli/... is green.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"5328120be55ab6af07a5cd35c7dc7f7a22f30f96","created_at":"2026-04-18T06:50:16.477597182Z","description":"## Smallest useful slice of capability gating (sibling of ddx-4817edfd).

Routing currently does not check whether the resolved model's context window can hold the bead prompt. A 32k-context fuzzy match against an 80k-token bead prompt routes 'successfully' and fails mid-dispatch with a provider 400 or truncated output.

## Scope

- Add optional EstimatedPromptTokens (uint32) to RouteRequest (cli/internal/agent/types.go).
- In evaluateCandidate (cli/internal/agent/routing.go), when EstimatedPromptTokens is set and > 0, reject (or penalize) candidates whose concrete_model context window is below it.
- Source of context window per candidate: v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits first, then catalog ModelEntry.ContextWindow (cli/internal/agent/catalog.go) as fallback. If neither is known, treat as 'unknown' — do NOT reject (avoid false negatives), but log at -v.
- Caller side: pass EstimatedPromptTokens from execute-bead when the bead prompt is assembled (best-effort — can use char/4 heuristic if a tokenizer is unavailable).

## Out of scope

- Tool-calling, structured-output, effort, permissions gating (separate sibling beads).
- Adding a tokenizer dependency. char/4 estimate is fine for v1.

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/types.go
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go
- cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go (or wherever the bead prompt is assembled)","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-f5cec42a","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing"],"notes":"Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing","parent":"ddx-4817edfd","priority":2,"status":"closed","title":"routing: context-window gating in evaluateCandidate","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:43:47.481306052Z"}

Covers server-and-planning-contracts, agent-execution-and-routing, web-ui-and-playwright-coverage, host-runtime-state-and-storage, and tracker-governance scope areas.","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-a6d2da0e-20260414T052012-77b85dc6-7743c4c8","issue_type":"epic","labels":["helix","kind:planning","action:align","area:docs","area:api","area:agent","area:ui","area:server"],"priority":0,"status":"closed","title":"align: server-plan evolution review epic","updated_at":"2026-04-14T05:25:08.417878843Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:137:{"archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-15T01:13:31Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3766264","closing_commit_sha":"604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0","created_at":"2026-04-15T01:11:17.497753676Z","description":"The embedded 'agent' harness can complete a task successfully (commit produced, tests pass) but then fail to terminate, getting stuck in a compaction retry loop with compaction always failing. The parent execute-loop never observes task_succeeded and hangs forever.

## Observed

Running 'ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --harness agent --model qwen/qwen3-coder-next' against axon-43ed769b (axon auth-core-types bead):

1. qwen3-coder-next ran the task correctly, producing commit 91d4bdb with 636 LOC of types + 109 green tests + clippy clean. The commit was present in the worktree at /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-axon-43ed769b-20260415T005652-ebe7a16a.

2. Instead of terminating, the agent kept generating events. After ~20 minutes the session log was 33 MB (8461+ events, 249 messages) and stuck in:

    {"type":"compaction.start",...}
    {"type":"compaction.end",...,"data":{"messages_after":249,"messages_before":249,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
    {"type":"compaction.start",...}
    {"type":"compaction.end",...,"data":{"messages_after":249,"messages_before":249,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
    ...

3. Parent ddx process in __futex_wait. Had to SIGTERM the parent (PID 4141811) and manually fast-forward-merge the worktree commit to land the work.

4. A1 (previous bead) ran the same harness/model and terminated correctly in ~14 min with identical-looking commit shape. The difference was bead size: A2's output was a single 633-line Rust file, vs A1's 350-line SQL + 290-line test file. A2 didn't exceed what A1 did — so the context-limit explanation doesn't fully account for it.

## Expected

- After the agent commits and has nothing more to do, it should emit task_succeeded and terminate
- Compaction should either succeed (messages_after < messages_before) or bail out cleanly, not retry indefinitely
- If the context genuinely cannot be compacted, the agent should report a structured failure and preserve the commits, not spin

## Scope

- Detect mid-turn compaction returning success=false repeatedly and bail with a structured 'compaction_stuck' status
- Bound the total number of compaction attempts in a single turn
- Ensure task termination happens immediately after a successful commit + no pending tool calls
- Add a watchdog at the execute-loop level: kill the child if no commit+progress event is observed within a budget

## Evidence

- Session log: /Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)
- Rescued commit: 91d4bdb on axon master
- Parent process: PID 4141811 (killed)

## Acceptance

- [ ] Compaction retry loop is bounded and fails structurally
- [ ] Post-commit termination path is deterministic
- [ ] Watchdog detects and terminates stuck sessions with a preserved-commit outcome
- [ ] Regression test reproduces the stuck state (seeded session with large no-op tool calls)","events_attachment":"ddx-25a41f51/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-15T01:39:31.55993889Z","id":"ddx-25a41f51","issue_type":"bug","labels":["helix","area:agent","area:exec","kind:bug","gap:stability"],"owner":"ddx","priority":0,"session_id":"eb-e80e780a","status":"closed","title":"bug: embedded agent stalls in post-commit compaction loop","updated_at":"2026-04-15T03:46:00.206978601Z"}

Both reviews flagged the same missing concept: routing currently scores candidates on cost class, quota, and historical success rate — but does NOT gate on whether the resolved route can actually execute the requested work. Concrete failure modes this lets through:

1. **Context-window overflow.** Bead prompt is 80k tokens. Fuzzy match picks a 32k-context model on the pinned provider. Routing 'succeeds'. Dispatch fails mid-stream with a provider 400 or truncated output.

2. **Missing tool-calling support.** Some omlx flavors or older local servers don't implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Routing picks them via fuzzy match. Dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' or silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop can't use.

3. **Missing structured-output support.** Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / tool-use-required. If the bead prompt relies on it, routing-level blindness means the failure only surfaces at dispatch.

4. **Effort / permissions.** --effort high or --permissions unrestricted may not be honored by every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and fail.

Codex: 'routing currently scores mostly on cost/quota/history, not whether a provider/model can satisfy tool calling, effort controls, permissions, or context-window needs.'
Opus: 'If the bead prompt is 80k tokens and the fuzzy-matched model is 32k, routing succeeds and dispatch fails mid-stream. Omlx flavor may not support tools at all.'

## Out of scope for this bead

This bead tracks the CONCEPT as a sibling of the main autorouting epic, but a full capability-constraint scoring system is a larger change than the current Phase-1 scope. Per opus: 'Scope this out of the epic explicitly (don't expand), but add a sibling bead so it's tracked.'

Split out into sub-scope bites is TBD:

1. **Context-window gating** — smallest useful slice. RouteRequest gains an optional EstimatedPromptTokens field; evaluateCandidate rejects (or penalizes) candidates whose concrete_model's known context window is below that. Uses v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits + catalog ContextWindow fallback.

2. **Tool-calling support gating** — needs per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata. Likely populated from discovery (/v1/models/status) + hand-curated capability table for harnesses.

3. **Effort / permissions gating** — mostly already in place for harnesses (Harness.EffortFlag checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable) but provider-specific gating (e.g., 'openrouter doesn't honor effort on non-reasoning models') is not.

4. **Structured-output support** — probably lowest priority; most reasoning-tool-capable models also do structured output.

This bead's AC is to file the sub-beads (smaller, focused) and close THIS bead once the sub-beads exist. The epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on those sub-beads for its exit gate — context-window etc. are a Phase 2 concern. The epic is about correct resolution; capability gating is about preventing resolved-but-unrunnable routes.

## Files touched (by sub-beads, not this one)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — CandidatePlan capability fields, RouteRequest capability hints
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — capability checks in rejectIfNotViable + scoring

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:381:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer opening a bead with a long id, the id truncates cleanly without overlapping any action button, the full id is discoverable on hover and copyable with one click, and the header buttons remain fully clickable.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. The bead-id span in `BeadDetail.svelte` uses `truncate min-w-0` (or equivalent) instead of `shrink-0`. The parent flex container keeps `min-w-0`.
2. The span has `title={bead.id}` so hovering reveals the full value.
3. A copy-to-clipboard icon button sits immediately after the id. Clicking copies the full id to the clipboard and surfaces a subtle confirmation (existing toast / inline check mark — whichever matches project conventions).
4. Action buttons (Claim/Unclaim, Edit, Delete, Close) remain fully visible and clickable regardless of id length. Status pill and owner chip remain visible; they may wrap below the id on narrow viewports but never overlap the action group.
5. Playwright e2e:
   - Seeds a bead with a 60-char id (e.g. `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-526efaf1`) and opens its detail panel.
   - Asserts the id element has a truncation class and that its rendered width is ≤ the parent container width minus the action-button group width.
   - Asserts the Delete button is clickable (`.click()` does not throw) and that its bounding rect does not intersect the id span's bounding rect.
   - Asserts hovering the id shows a tooltip (or reveals `title` attribute) with the full value, and clicking the copy button writes the full id to the clipboard.
6. Unit/visual: at `max-w-xl` width the header renders without horizontal scroll and without clipped action buttons for ids of length 8, 32, 60, and 120.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-24T06:44:49Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"196235","closing_commit_sha":"a3a9887a4a5b098e2e986dff13d3b514c7b476f5","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:52:31.873838627Z","description":"## Observed

On the bead detail panel, a long bead id overflows and visually overlaps the Claim/Unclaim/Edit/Delete/Close buttons on the right. Reporter example: `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-526efaf1` (~60 chars).

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/components/BeadDetail.svelte:211-221` —

```svelte
<div class="flex shrink-0 items-center justify-between border-b ... px-6 py-4">
  <div class="flex min-w-0 items-center gap-3">
    <span class="shrink-0 font-mono text-xs ...">{bead.id}</span>       ← shrink-0 prevents truncation
    <span class="shrink-0 font-medium ...">{bead.status}</span>
    {#if bead.owner}
      <span class="truncate text-xs ...">@ {bead.owner}</span>
    {/if}
  </div>
  <div class="ml-3 flex shrink-0 items-center gap-2">
    [Claim/Unclaim] [Edit] [Delete] [Close]
  </div>
</div>
```

The bead-id span has `shrink-0`, so it never truncates regardless of length. The outer panel is `max-w-xl` (576px). A 60-char monospace id occupies roughly 400-500px on its own; combined with the status pill, owner chip, and the action-button group (also `shrink-0`), it pushes past the available width. Flex layout collapses by overlapping rather than wrapping, so the id draws on top of the buttons.

## Proposed direction

1. **Replace `shrink-0` on the bead-id span with `truncate min-w-0`.** The parent `<div class="flex min-w-0 items-center gap-3">` already establishes a shrinkable container.
2. **Add `title={bead.id}` so hovering shows the full value** (important — truncating monospace ids from the right loses the distinguishing suffix).
3. **Add a copy-to-clipboard icon button** next to the id so operators can grab the full value with one click when the id is truncated. Small icon button, no extra visual weight.
4. **Sanity check:** the reporter's example starts with `.` and has the shape of an execute-bead worktree name. Worth briefly checking whether something is producing such a value as a bead id (worktree name leaking into bead creation?) — but that's a separate investigation. The UI still needs to degrade gracefully regardless.

## Out of scope

- Changing bead id generation or enforcing max length on ids.
- Redesign of the detail-panel header layout.","events_attachment":"ddx-71fbe968/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-24T06:48:19.884413094Z","execute-loop-last-detail":"escalation exhausted: unsupported model "minimax/minimax-m2.7" for harness "gemini"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","feature":"FEAT-008","id":"ddx-71fbe968","issue_type":"bug","labels":["feat-008","feat-004","ui","css"],"owner":"ddx","priority":3,"session_id":"eb-78023e02","status":"closed","title":"Bead detail header: long bead ids overlap action buttons (shrink-0 prevents truncation)","updated_at":"2026-04-24T06:48:58.508228919Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:411:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, I can see every execute-bead worktree on my project, understand which ones are active vs completed vs abandoned, and clean up abandoned ones with confirmation without shelling out to git commands.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Inventory resolver.** `executeBeadWorktrees(projectId)` returns structured entries as described. Unit test covers: (a) active worktree with live worker, (b) completed worktree with matching execution bundle and head==result_rev, (c) abandoned worktree past the threshold, (d) orphan (path without git registration).

2. **State classification.** `state` is computed deterministically from inputs (workers list, execution bundles, git worktree list, filesystem stat, configured threshold). Classifier is a single pure function with table-driven tests covering all four states and at least two edge cases per state.

3. **UI — list view.** `/worktrees` route renders a table with columns path/branch/bead/execution/state/created/last-activity/disk. Sortable by any column. Filters: state, bead. Default sort: newest `last activity` first.

4. **UI — actions.** Per-row: Remove (confirm-gated), Force-remove (for orphans, extra-confirm-gated with typed confirmation of the path). Both actions call through to git + filesystem via a mutation; neither is performed client-side.

5. **CLI — prune.** `ddx server state worktree-prune [--dry-run] [--project <path>]` lists abandoned+orphan worktrees and prompts for removal (unless `--yes`). Dry-run prints the list without touching anything. Integration test runs against a fixture repo with one of each state and asserts only the expected ones are targeted.

6. **Sweep + notice.** Server background sweep runs at a configurable interval (default 15m), logs a summary, and exposes a GraphQL counter (`executeBeadWorktreeCounters { total, active, abandoned, orphan }`) for dashboard rollup. Sweep never deletes unless `worktrees.auto_prune: true` is set in config.

7. **Cross-links.**
   - From a worktree row, click execution → navigates to `/executions/<id>` (depends on `ddx-726bc5f2`).
   - From an execution detail page, a "Worktree" section shows the worktree (if still present) with the same state badge.

8. **Fixture + perf.** Per standing directive in `ddx-9ce6842a`, seed a fixture with ≥100 worktrees (mix of states). List view p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP. This is small-n territory; should be well within filesystem-scan limits, no DB substrate expected.

9. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds fixture with one worktree of each of the four states.
   - Navigates to `/worktrees`, asserts all four rows with correct state badges.
   - Clicks Remove on an abandoned worktree, confirms, asserts the row disappears within 2s and the worktree is gone from `git worktree list`.
   - Attempts Remove on an active worktree, asserts the confirm dialog warns and the action is gated.

10. **Path convention decision note.** Before Part 4 lands, a one-page note in `docs/helix/02-design/` records the canonical location choice and the migration plan. Parts 1-3 may ship without this note; Part 4 requires it.

11. **Cross-references.** Notes link to: `ddx-12cae4dd` (documents noise), `ddx-71fbe968` (long id overlap — same worktree-name leak into UI), `ddx-726bc5f2` (executions UI — worktrees complement it), `ddx-15f7ee0b` (project registry cleanup pattern reused here).","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"6449424a64780692a3295a7a061db87d52f17eb8","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:56:37.613502322Z","description":"## Observed

`git worktree list` on the current ddx repo shows **39 worktrees**:
- 1 main worktree (the clone itself).
- ~37 under `.claude/worktrees/agent-<hash>/` from execute-bead runs. Branch names `worktree-agent-<hash>`.
- At least 1 under `/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-<beadId>-<ts>-<hash>` (detached HEAD) — different path convention, different semantics.

There is no UI visibility into any of this. A user cannot see which worktrees exist, what bead each one belongs to, whether each is active/abandoned/completed, or clean them up short of `git worktree prune` + `rm -rf`. The `.claude/worktrees/` entries also leak into other surfaces (already tracked):
- Documents view noise (`ddx-12cae4dd`).
- One was surviving in the project registry cleanup earlier today (`proj-4c1ca90e: agent-a7bf6d44`).

## Why the two path conventions?

- `.claude/worktrees/agent-<hash>` — created with a branch (`worktree-agent-<hash>`), likely by interactive execute-bead runs or an earlier worktree convention.
- `/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-<bead>-<ts>-<hash>` — detached HEAD, under `/tmp`, convention that ships with current execute-bead (this is what's producing the long "bead id"-shaped names that leaked into UI in `ddx-71fbe968`).

Two conventions is itself a smell: inconsistent path discipline makes cleanup harder and makes cross-referencing an execution bundle → worktree unreliable.

## What's needed

### Part 1 — Inventory resolver

- GraphQL: `executeBeadWorktrees(projectId)` → list of `{path, branch, beadId?, executionId?, baseRev, headRev, state, createdAt, lastActivityAt}`.
- Source: `git worktree list --porcelain` + filesystem stat + manifest lookup for executions whose `execution_dir` matches or whose path correlates.
- `state` ∈ `{active, completed, abandoned, orphan}`:
  - **active** — an execute-bead session is currently running against this worktree (cross-reference workers / sessions).
  - **completed** — associated execution bundle exists with a verdict; head matches `result_rev`.
  - **abandoned** — no recent activity (configurable, default 24h), no matching running worker, but worktree still present.
  - **orphan** — path on disk but `git worktree list` does not know about it (or vice versa).

### Part 2 — Sidebar + route

- New "Worktrees" view, or slot this into the Executions detail (`ddx-726bc5f2`) as a sidebar panel since every worktree is for some execution. Design call; recommend a dedicated view so orphans (which have no execution) are still visible.
- Route: `/nodes/<n>/projects/<p>/worktrees`.
- Columns: path, branch, bead (link), execution (link), state, created, last activity, disk usage.
- Row actions: **Open execution**, **Remove worktree** (confirm-gated; calls `git worktree remove` and cleans up `.claude/worktrees/<name>`), **Force remove** (for orphans; `-f`).

### Part 3 — Policy + cleanup

- Background sweep (server-side, already has a sweep pattern from project registry): every N minutes, list worktrees, detect abandoned state, emit a notice (but **never auto-delete** — user-confirmed cleanup only by default).
- `ddx server state worktree-prune [--dry-run]` CLI command that enumerates and optionally removes abandoned worktrees. Same deletion path as the UI action.
- Config: `.ddx/config.yaml: worktrees.abandoned_after: 24h`, `worktrees.auto_prune: false`. Opt-in auto-prune for teams that want it.

### Part 4 — Path convention unification

- Decide a single canonical location for execute-bead worktrees. Recommend `.claude/worktrees/<bead>-<ts>-<hash>/` or a sibling `.ddx/worktrees/` — **pick one and stick with it**.
- Migrate existing paths lazily: on first sweep after upgrade, record the path as-is and leave it; new worktrees use the new convention. Old ones phase out as they complete or get cleaned.
- This is a separable sub-effort; acceptable to land Parts 1-3 against the current mixed reality and do Part 4 afterward.

## Out of scope

- Changing how execute-bead creates worktrees (beyond the canonical-location choice in Part 4).
- Documents-view noise — covered by `ddx-12cae4dd`.
- Long-id display overlap — covered by `ddx-71fbe968`.","events_attachment":"ddx-7aa810b8/events.jsonl","execute-loop-last-detail":"escalation exhausted: unsupported model "minimax/minimax-m2.7" for harness "gemini"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","execute-loop-no-changes-count":1,"feature":"FEAT-010","id":"ddx-7aa810b8","issue_type":"task","labels":["feat-008","feat-006","feat-010","worktrees","hygiene"],"priority":2,"session_id":"eb-aff6cbca","status":"closed","title":"Track execute-bead worktree lifecycle: 39 worktrees on one repo, no UI visibility, no cleanup policy","updated_at":"2026-04-24T03:17:31.259606375Z"}

Its ID is referenced in this bead's events.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-30T00:56:04Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2278885","closing_commit_sha":"e1e5f7cfd8c9656260461a7e63d3e67883bdd77a","created_at":"2026-04-29T19:05:33.7239674Z","description":"## Two-layer problem

### Layer 1 (mechanical bug)

When a worker invokes `ddx bead create` from inside its execute-bead worktree, the resulting bead IDs are malformed. Reproduced today on this project:

  /home/erik/.local/bin/ddx bead ready  → 11 entries with IDs like
  `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-44236615-20260429T185004-ffa9adf1-e630aeae`

These were the read-coverage child beads spawned from the ddx-44236615 worker (commit ebeb2464). They have valid descriptions, AC, priorities, and labels — only the ID field is mangled. The pattern `.execute-bead-wt-<parent>-<timestamp>-<short>-<random>` matches the worktree directory name format used by `ddx agent execute-bead`. Most likely failure path: the bead-id resolver consults `os.Getwd()` (or equivalent) to derive a project context prefix, sees the worktree dir, and uses it instead of the real project root + a fresh `ddx-<8hex>` short hash.

Concrete impact:
- 50+ char IDs are unwieldy in CLI commands (`ddx bead show <id>`, `ddx bead dep add ...`).
- Cross-project readers and the bead store assume `ddx-<8hex>` prefix matching for tooling (filtering, GraphQL adapters, MCP responses) — these long IDs may sort or filter unexpectedly.
- The IDs encode ephemeral attempt timestamps, which leaks worker-internal state into a permanent identifier.
- 12 such beads exist in the tracker as of this filing (grep `.execute-bead-wt-` in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`).

### Layer 2 (design question — separate decision)

Even if the IDs were correctly formed `ddx-<8hex>`, it is not obvious that `ddx bead create` from inside an execute-bead worker is the right pattern at all. Two alternatives the design has not articulated a position on:

**(a) Append to the parent bead.** When the worker discovers sub-tasks, it appends them as structured items in the parent's description, notes, or a new `kind:discovered-subtask` event. The parent bead becomes the durable record of "what this run found"; future runs (or the operator) can decompose if/when the work warrants its own tracker entries.

**(b) Surface via the execute-bead result.** The agent emits a structured `discovered_subtasks: [...]` field on result.json; the loop / operator decides whether to file new beads. Keeps tracker mutations gated by an explicit decision point.

**(c) Create new beads in-band (current behavior).** The worker calls `ddx bead create` directly. Pro: fastest decomposition. Con: sub-tasks land in the queue without operator review; can flood the queue if the agent over-decomposes; complicates "what was THIS run's contribution" auditing.

Today's behavior is (c) but undocumented. The 11 read-coverage children landing as a single agent's decomposition is exactly the failure mode (c) makes easy: one analysis bead spawned 11 implementation beads in a single run, all P0, all without operator review. The work may be valid — but the pattern needs a position.

## In-scope outcomes

This bead's job is to surface both layers; the implementation work decomposes into follow-ups.

1. **Concrete fix for layer 1**: bead-id resolver should normalize worktree paths to the real project root before allocating an ID. Test: `ddx bead create` invoked with cwd=`<repo>/.execute-bead-wt-...` produces `ddx-<8hex>`, not `.execute-bead-wt-...-<random>`.
2. **Mass repair for the 12 existing malformed beads**: either rewrite their IDs to fresh `ddx-<8hex>` (with a redirect or alias map for any external references), or close them as deferred and re-file with proper IDs. Decision deferred until layer 2 is settled.
3. **Layer 2 decision**: file a separate design bead (or amendment to FEAT-006 / FEAT-010) that picks (a), (b), or (c) and documents the rationale. This bead does NOT pre-decide; it captures the question.

## In-scope files (layer 1 fix)

- `cli/cmd/bead_create.go` (or wherever bead-create resolves project context).
- `cli/internal/bead/store.go` or `cli/internal/bead/id.go` if ID generation lives there.
- A unit test that drops cwd into a worktree-shaped dir and asserts the resulting bead id matches `^ddx-[0-9a-f]{8}$`.

(Locate via: `grep -rn "execute-bead-wt\|FindProjectRoot\|projectRoot" cli/internal/bead/ cli/cmd/bead_*.go`.)

## Out of scope

- The 11 read-coverage child beads' actual implementation work — that's separate beads.
- Default behavior change for whether workers can/should create beads — that's the layer-2 design bead.
- GraphQL or MCP changes to handle long IDs — fix the source instead.

## Acceptance

Layer-1 only (this bead):

1. `cd cli && go test ./cmd/... ./internal/bead/...` passes after the fix.
2. New unit test in `cli/internal/bead/` (or `cli/cmd/`) that simulates a bead-create call from `cwd=<project>/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-XXXXX-<timestamp>-<random>` and asserts the new bead's `id` field matches `^ddx-[0-9a-f]{8}$`. Test fails before the fix, passes after.
3. `grep -E '"id":"\.execute-bead-wt-' .ddx/beads.jsonl` returns either 0 (mass repair) or 12 with a recorded decision in this bead's notes that the existing 12 are deferred to a follow-up bead.
4. A separate bead is filed (or referenced existing one updated) that captures the layer-2 design question (a/b/c). Reference its ID in this bead's events.

## Why one bead, not an epic

Layer 1 is a tight 1-3 file fix. Layer 2 is a design discussion that should be its own artifact, not a sibling task. Splitting them keeps each bounded.","events_attachment":"ddx-7eab13a6/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-30T00:59:34.510624284Z","id":"ddx-7eab13a6","issue_type":"bug","labels":["area:bead","area:agent","kind:bug","kind:design"],"notes":"Layer-1 (ID resolver bug) fixed in this bead: detectPrefix() now takes a workingDir parameter and uses it for git commands instead of defaulting to cwd. The WorkerManager in cli/internal/server/workers.go already tracks this (WorkerRecord.CurrentBead at line 51, WorkerRecord.State, WorkerRecord.Harness, WorkerRecord.Model, WorkerRecord.Attempts/Successes/Failures) but it is only accessible through the server API. This bead adds ddx agent workers as a top-level CLI subcommand. It must not depend on worktrunk (wt) or any external tool.

## Required changes

### 1. New command: ddx agent workers (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)
Register alongside the existing ddx agent log command. Flags:
  --json         emit raw JSON array
  --watch        re-render every 2s (like watch -n2) until Ctrl-C
  --project      filter to a specific project root (default: CWD project)

### 2. Data sources (two paths)
A. Server-submitted workers: GET http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agent/workers — deserialize []WorkerRecord from the server. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.
B. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:
   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)
   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, harness, model, started_at

### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan
For each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:
  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)
  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)
  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available
  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields

### 4. Table output (default)
Columns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | HARNESS | MODEL | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS
- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)
- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)
- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID

### 5. Merge and deduplicate
When both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.

## Key files
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()
- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)
- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)","events_attachment":"ddx-9060c8b2/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-14T07:45:03.73510309Z","id":"ddx-9060c8b2","issue_type":"task","labels":["ux","execute-loop","observability","cli"],"owner":"ddx","priority":1,"session_id":"eb-reconcile-3f16c49","status":"closed","title":"Add ddx agent workers command — native live worker status with current bead","updated_at":"2026-04-14T14:12:47.499704451Z"}

`ddx agent execute-loop` selected and launched an open `epic` bead from the HELIX execution queue instead of a bounded executable bead.

## Reproduction
1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`, run `ddx bead ready --json --execution`.
2. Observe the first candidate is `helix-fef22846`, `issue_type=epic`, title `Audit deleted HELIX artifact types for restoration or retirement`.
3. Run `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json`.
4. Observe DDx creates `.ddx/.execute-bead-wt-helix-fef22846-20260411T011535-bbda19d7` and `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/manifest.json` with `bead_id=helix-fef22846`.

## Observed gap
`ReadyExecution()` currently treats any open, unblocked bead as execution-ready unless `execution-eligible=false`, `superseded-by`, or cooldown metadata says otherwise. `execute-loop` then takes the first candidate without a stronger structural execution screen. That behavior is weaker than the API-001 execute-bead supervisor contract, which says the loop should run a generic execution-ready validator against ordered candidates before launch.

## Why this matters
Open epics represent umbrella planning/decomposition work, not necessarily a bounded workspace transform. Launching them directly makes queue-drain behavior unstable and can strand the loop on non-executable work even when bounded tasks are ready behind the epic.","events_attachment":"ddx-b44ca394/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-14T03:39:25.89063588Z","execute-loop-last-detail":"agent execution failed","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","execute-loop-retry-after":"2026-04-13T21:32:10Z","execution-eligible":true,"id":"ddx-b44ca394","issue_type":"bug","labels":["helix","phase:build","area:agent","area:bead","area:cli"],"notes":"Blocker ddx-cf340665 is closed. .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:717:{"archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-29T23:22:59Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"932496","closing_commit_sha":"ad42d24fcb5f39f4ab8c43e68a729b6abe466822","created_at":"2026-04-29T22:00:58.431696222Z","description":"During investigation of ddx-7eab13a6 (bead-id resolver worktree bug), a design question surfaced: is it correct for execute-bead workers to call `ddx bead create` directly in-band?

Three alternatives:

(a) Append to parent bead. Worker appends discovered sub-tasks as structured items in the parent's notes or a new discovered-subtask event. The parent becomes the durable record; future runs or the operator can decompose.

(b) Surface via result. Agent emits a structured discovered_subtasks: [...] field in result.json; the execute-loop / operator decides whether to file new beads. Keeps tracker mutations gated by an explicit decision point.

(c) Create beads in-band (current behavior). Worker calls ddx bead create directly. Pro: fastest decomposition. Con: sub-tasks land in the queue without operator review; can flood the queue on over-decomposition.

Today's behavior is (c) but undocumented. One worker spawned 11 read-coverage children in a single run (ddx-44236615), all P0, without operator review. The work may be valid but the pattern needs a documented position.

Additionally, 22 existing beads have malformed IDs of the form .execute-bead-wt-<parent>-<timestamp>-<random>-<hex> (caused by the layer-1 bug fixed in ddx-7eab13a6). A mass-repair or close+refile decision should be made once the design position is set.

Scope: pick (a), (b), or (c) and document in a spec amendment (FEAT-006 or FEAT-010 if applicable); record the rationale. Also decide whether to repair the 22 existing malformed-ID beads.","events_attachment":"ddx-d3a4f89c/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-29T23:25:29.863545721Z","id":"ddx-d3a4f89c","issue_type":"task","labels":["area:bead","area:agent","kind:design"],"owner":"erik","priority":2,"session_id":"eb-9d8046f9","status":"closed","title":"layer-2 design: should execute-bead workers create beads in-band?","updated_at":"2026-04-29T23:26:16.710773437Z"}

AC4. `ddx agent workers --json` includes a 'liveness' or 'pid_alive' field per entry so operators can see at a glance which entries are live vs stale, even before pruning.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-29T17:59:55Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"932496","closing_commit_sha":"07ae8022f9f55ba3fecc2378ae28e2e0eabf0cad","created_at":"2026-04-29T13:14:56.008884527Z","description":"When a local execute-bead/execute-loop process exits abnormally (killed, OOM, parent shell terminated, container crash) the worker entry can remain in the in-memory registry forever. `ddx agent workers` lists it as state=running with elapsed_ms continuously advancing, even though no PID exists.

Reproducing today (axon repo): worker local-20260429T030828-075a28c5 (bead hx-5f4d4ea1, harness=agent, model=qwen3.6) started at 03:08 UTC, has logged elapsed=10+ hours, and `ps -ef | grep execute-bead` shows nothing. The git worktree at /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-hx-5f4d4ea1-* has only the checkpoint commit (no actual work commits), confirming the worker died early.

Available paths to clean up are all unsatisfying:
1. `ddx agent workers stop --bead hx-5f4d4ea1` returns 'no matching workers' (because the matcher only finds live workers)
2. `ddx agent workers stop <worker-id>` returns server error 400 'worker not running' (same contradiction — the registry says running, the stopper says not running)
3. `systemctl --user restart ddx-server` works but kills every other active worker on the box, including ones for other repos that are doing real work
4. Manual edit of server-state.json risks corruption and isn't documented

The bead itself wasn't blocked (status=open, owner=null) so the registry noise is mostly cosmetic — it pollutes `ddx agent workers` output and confuses operators trying to triage the queue. Description: The new close flow writes bead status first, auto-commits .ddx/beads.jsonl, and only then writes closing_commit_sha back to the store. In a clean temp git repo with git.auto_commit=always and DDX_BEAD_DIR unset, running 'ddx bead close <id>' leaves 'git status --short' dirty with ' M .ddx/beads.jsonl'. The landed HEAD commit 9494afc82408cac9a650d616fc3207197e1dfbd6 contains only the status=open->closed change, while the durable closing_commit_sha appears only in the uncommitted worktree diff. That means the command still fails to land close provenance atomically and leaves replay/audit metadata outside the recorded close commit.
Suggested fix: Restructure bead close so the recorded closing_commit_sha is present in the same landed commit that closes the bead, or perform and persist a second deterministic commit/update so git status is clean after close and HEAD contains the final provenance-bearing row.","id":"hx-73c95bab","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium"],"owner":"erik","parent":"hx-df089a3d","priority":0,"spec-id":"cli/cmd/bead.go","status":"closed","title":"integration: persist close provenance in the landed tracker commit","updated_at":"2026-04-09T23:42:54.706314959Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:1020:{"acceptance":"With git.auto_commit=always and DDX_BEAD_DIR unset, closing a bead whose landed implementation changes only tracked .ddx/* files still records closing_commit_sha for the landed close commit, and ddx agent replay can use that commit boundary instead of falling back to sessionless/manual mode.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T00:46:59Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3599950","closing_commit_sha":"9119c7cf3a95687f50ff3ed5d1f6353e502c74f6","created_at":"2026-04-10T00:29:53.073036931Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cli/cmd/bead.go:80
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 71f97be suppresses closing_commit_sha whenever the landed close commit touches only paths under .ddx/. That is too broad: .ddx contains real governed artifacts such as config and other tracked workspace files, not just tracker backfills. In a temp repo with git.auto_commit=always and DDX_BEAD_DIR unset, creating a bead, modifying a tracked .ddx/notes.txt file, and running ddx bead close lands a close commit that changes .ddx/beads.jsonl and .ddx/notes.txt, but the bead still omits closing_commit_sha. FEAT-019 only says to omit metadata-only tracker backfills; dropping provenance for legitimate .ddx-scoped work makes replay/audit fall back to sessionless mode even though the implementation boundary is available.
Suggested fix: Detect metadata-only close commits more narrowly, for example by distinguishing pure .ddx/beads.jsonl backfills from other tracked .ddx artifacts, and add regression coverage for a close commit that lands tracked .ddx work.","id":"hx-98fec407","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"cli/cmd/bead.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: preserve closing provenance for legitimate .ddx-only work","updated_at":"2026-04-10T00:48:20.400206217Z"}

Updated\n    ddx-b808df39 notes to cross-reference this investigation so the\n    operator stop/kill tool tracks the underlying fixes.\n\ndiff --git a/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md b/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md\nnew file mode 100644\nindex 00000000..5e8547cf\n--- /dev/null\n+++ b/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md\n@@ -0,0 +1,193 @@\n+# Investigation: root cause of 33–142h hung execute-bead workers\n+\n+**Bead:** ddx-0a651925 (investigates; sibling to ddx-b808df39)\n+**Attempt:** 20260418T043148-1346e8a3\n+**Scope:** Source-code + execution-bundle analysis. No live hung worker was available\n+in this isolated worktree to capture live `ps`/`pprof`, so the incident evidence is\n+drawn from `.ddx/executions/*` bundles and the worker/runner source path.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 1. Observed evidence (from `.ddx/executions/`)\n+\n+| Metric                                               | Count / value                                        |\n+| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |\n+| Total attempt bundles                                | 328                                                  |\n+| Bundles with `result.json`                           | 291 (89%)                                            |\n+| Bundles with `manifest.json` but **no** `result.json` | 37 (11%) — executions whose worker never finalized  |\n+\n+Top five longest *finalized* attempts:\n+\n+| `duration_ms` | Wall time    | `detail`                                                                    | Bundle                          |\n+| ------------- | ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |\n+| 7,720,908     | **2h 8m**    | `agent: compaction stuck: 50 consecutive failed compaction attempts`        | 20260416T230316-efbf91c5        |\n+| 7,200,098     | **2h 0m**    | `timeout after 2h0m0s` (claude streaming wall-clock fired)                  | 20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba        |\n+| 6,764,927     | **1h 53m**   | success (agent harness, qwen)                                               | 20260416T230315-f9f46822        |\n+| 5,649,987     | **1h 34m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260416T230313-e193942b        |\n+| 4,284,347     | **1h 11m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260415T103422-bb3a45fa        |\n+\n+**Key observation:** the 33h / 142h hang durations cited in ddx-b808df39 are\n+~15× and ~65× longer than the longest *finalized* attempt on record. This means\n+the hung workers never reached the point of writing `result.json` — they were\n+blocked inside `runner.Run` (or deeper) for days. Every code path that\n+*should* have terminated them (idle timeout, compaction-stuck, stall detector,\n+cancel propagation) failed to fire.\n+\n+The 37 manifest-only bundles are candidates for hung or externally-killed\n+workers; e.g. `20260416T215953-6b515c05` has a manifest from 2026-04-16T21:59Z\n+with no result. Those fit the profile of \"workers running 33h–142h with no bead\n+assigned\" reported on agent projects.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 2. Root-cause classification (source code evidence)\n+\n+The investigation checked the four hypotheses from the bead description.\n+\n+### RC1 (PRIMARY) — Caller context is discarded inside the Runner\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (`Runner.Run`):\n+```go\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+```\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (`Runner.RunAgent`):\n+```go\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+```\n+\n+`RunOptions` (types.go) takes no `context.Context` at all. `agent.ExecuteBead`\n+likewise does not accept ctx, and `server.WorkerManager.runWorker` passes its\n+own ctx into `worker.Run(ctx, …)` but that ctx **never reaches the agent\n+invocation**.\n+\n+**Impact:** `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (workers.go:648) calls `handle.cancel()`\n+which cancels only the worker goroutine's ctx. The running agent provider call\n+/ subprocess is detached. The only way to interrupt a stuck agent today is a\n+SIGKILL from outside the process. This is the structural defect that enables\n+33–142h hangs — once the agent is stuck, nothing inside DDx can stop it.\n+\n+### RC2 — Idle-reset timer, not wall-clock deadline\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/executor.go:35-40` creates an *idle timeout* context value:\n+```go\n+func withExecutionTimeout(ctx context.Context, timeout time.Duration) context.Context {\n+    return context.WithValue(ctx, executionTimeoutKey{}, timeout)\n+}\n+```\n+\n+`executor.go:154-178` consumes it and **resets the timer on every stdout/stderr\n+byte**. `agent_runner.go:264-285` implements the same pattern against agentlib\n+events.\n+\n+The default timeout is 2h (`types.go:363 DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000`). The\n+comment claims \"2 hours — long enough for any task; supervisor/agent decides\n+termination\" but the actual behaviour is **\"2 hours of silence required to fire\"**.\n+A provider that emits a heartbeat, a ping, a retry line, or any byte resets the\n+timer indefinitely.\n+\n+The `runClaudeStreaming` path (`claude_stream.go:373-384`) is the only path that\n+uses a wall-clock deadline — and it is the only path we see actually hitting\n+the timeout (bundle 20260415T015231 exactly 2h 0m 0.098s).\n+\n+**Impact:** for `harness=agent` (qwen, minimax, openrouter, etc.), no wall-clock\n+bound exists. 33–142h hangs are consistent with a provider that dribbles events\n+slowly enough to defeat the idle reset.\n+\n+### RC3 — Compaction-stuck breaker fires late\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:218-241` increments a counter on each no-op\n+compaction event and trips at `stuckThreshold = maxConsecutiveCompactionFailures`\n+(50 by default). On observed `efbf91c5` it took **2h 8m** to trip. If one\n+iteration takes 3 minutes to produce a no-op compaction event, 50 events =\n+~150 min — matches the observed tail.\n+\n+**Impact:** on its own, the breaker is too slow to be a useful hang-guard. It\n+should be replaced or complemented by a wall-clock-bounded variant.\n+\n+### RC4 — Provider HTTP requests likely lack a per-request deadline\n+\n+`RunAgent` passes the internal `context.Background()`-derived ctx into\n+`agentlib.Run(ctx, req)`. The agent library's providers (`openai.Client`,\n+anthropic client) do not appear to install a per-request deadline themselves\n+(grepping the v0.3.14 module cache shows short timeouts on discovery/probe\n+only; the main call path has no WithTimeout). A stalled TCP socket after\n+headers are received but before the response body arrives can hang a streaming\n+read indefinitely.\n+\n+**Impact:** consistent with \"alive but mid-LLM-call with no client-side\n+deadline\" — hypothesis (2) in the bead description. Cannot be fully confirmed\n+from source alone; needs runtime `go tool pprof http://localhost:<port>/debug/pprof/goroutine`\n+against a live hung worker.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — BashTool subprocess hang (hypothesis 1)\n+\n+Not the root cause. `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14/tool/bash.go:15`\n+installs a 120s default wall-clock timeout (`context.WithTimeout(ctx, timeout)`)\n+and calls `exec.CommandContext`. Per-call bash hangs self-terminate at 2min.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — Tracker-side claim leak (hypothesis 3)\n+\n+Tracker state is incidental to the hang: the bead's own description notes the\n+hung workers exist as processes (`ps aux | grep claude`). The worker process is\n+alive; the claim is alive because the worker is alive. Fixing the claim alone\n+does not kill the worker. This hypothesis is downstream, not upstream.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — Worktree file-lock (hypothesis 4)\n+\n+No evidence in bundles. Execute-bead worktrees are plain git worktrees at\n+`/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/*`; no process-held file locks would survive across 33–142h\n+without the owning process. If the process is alive, the lock is alive — same\n+as RC3.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 3. Relationship to ddx-b808df39\n+\n+ddx-b808df39 proposes an operator `ddx agent workers stop <id>` command that\n+sends **SIGTERM at the OS level**, then SIGKILL after a grace period. That\n+design **does** work around RC1 (process-level signal bypasses the broken ctx\n+plumbing). So ddx-b808df39 is a *correct mitigation* — but only a mitigation.\n+\n+Without fixing RC1 (and at least RC2), operators will keep needing to invoke\n+`workers stop` manually. The autonomous remediations every other system relies\n+on (cancel ctx, idle timeout, circuit breaker) are each broken today in ways\n+that require explicit code changes, not just a new operator tool.\n+\n+**Recommendation:** Ship ddx-b808df39 first (quick operational relief), then\n+resolve the child beads filed below in order RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 4. Follow-up beads filed\n+\n+Each addresses one root cause with a deterministic acceptance test. Priority\n+and ID are shown; full details available via `ddx bead show <id>`.\n+\n+The IDs below carry an `.execute-bead-wt-…` prefix because `ddx bead create`\n+was invoked from inside the isolated execute-bead worktree — this is a\n+separate DDx bug worth noting but orthogonal to the hang investigation. They\n+are parented to `ddx-0a651925` and resolvable by `ddx bead list --parent ddx-0a651925`.\n+\n+| RC  | Priority | Bead ID (suffix)                                                           | Title                                                              |\n+| --- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |\n+| RC1 | P0       | `…-526efaf1` | propagate caller context through Runner.Run and RunAgent           |\n+| RC2 | P0       | `…-b203c889` | add wall-clock deadline alongside idle timeout for agent harness   |\n+| RC3 | P2       | `…-b0562380` | bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock time                  |\n+| RC4 | P1       | `…-3e3913a0` | audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent provider calls |\n+| —   | P1       | `…-34d039b7` | autonomous worker watchdog with process-level reaper (complements ddx-b808df39) |\n+\n+**Suggested execution order:** RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3 → watchdog. RC1 is a\n+prerequisite for RC4 being meaningful.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 5. What this investigation could NOT do\n+\n+- Capture live `ps`/`pprof` from a hung worker — none was accessible from the\n+  isolated execute-bead worktree. RC4 in particular needs a live `go tool pprof`\n+  goroutine dump to confirm that the stuck goroutine is in `net/http` streaming\n+  read, not some other path.\n+- Confirm whether the agent library's providers install any timeout that was\n+  missed in grep. The listed RC4 bead should start with a provider-path audit\n+  rather than an immediate fix.","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"7c4457b5-7817-4bd4-9850-94282c2cfdb7","uuid":"b9b08566-e074-49f2-a31f-e547a318b057","timestamp":"2026-04-18T04:51:23.303Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"commit 13cd213e157cd211c48f51796bd62a2fb8b1fcae\nAuthor: Test User <test@ddx.test>\nDate:   Sat Apr 18 00:49:37 2026 -0400\n\n    docs(agent): investigate root cause of 33–142h hung workers [ddx-0a651925]\n    \n    Root-cause investigation report for hung execute-bead workers observed on\n    agent projects (33h–142h). Root-cause classification (source code evidence)\n+\n+The investigation checked the four hypotheses from the bead description.\n+\n+### RC1 (PRIMARY) — Caller context is discarded inside the Runner\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (`Runner.Run`):\n+```go\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+```\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (`Runner.RunAgent`):\n+```go\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+```\n+\n+`RunOptions` (types.go) takes no `context.Context` at all. `agent.ExecuteBead`\n+likewise does not accept ctx, and `server.WorkerManager.runWorker` passes its\n+own ctx into `worker.Run(ctx, …)` but that ctx **never reaches the agent\n+invocation**.\n+\n+**Impact:** `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (workers.go:648) calls `handle.cancel()`\n+which cancels only the worker goroutine's ctx. The running agent provider call\n+/ subprocess is detached. The only way to interrupt a stuck agent today is a\n+SIGKILL from outside the process. This is the structural defect that enables\n+33–142h hangs — once the agent is stuck, nothing inside DDx can stop it.\n+\n+### RC2 — Idle-reset timer, not wall-clock deadline\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/executor.go:35-40` creates an *idle timeout* context value:\n+```go\n+func withExecutionTimeout(ctx context.Context, timeout time.Duration) context.Context {\n+    return context.WithValue(ctx, executionTimeoutKey{}, timeout)\n+}\n+```\n+\n+`executor.go:154-178` consumes it and **resets the timer on every stdout/stderr\n+byte**. `agent_runner.go:264-285` implements the same pattern against agentlib\n+events.\n+\n+The default timeout is 2h (`types.go:363 DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000`). The\n+comment claims \"2 hours — long enough for any task; supervisor/agent decides\n+termination\" but the actual behaviour is **\"2 hours of silence required to fire\"**.\n+A provider that emits a heartbeat, a ping, a retry line, or any byte resets the\n+timer indefinitely.\n+\n+The `runClaudeStreaming` path (`claude_stream.go:373-384`) is the only path that\n+uses a wall-clock deadline — and it is the only path we see actually hitting\n+the timeout (bundle 20260415T015231 exactly 2h 0m 0.098s).\n+\n+**Impact:** for `harness=agent` (qwen, minimax, openrouter, etc.), no wall-clock\n+bound exists. 33–142h hangs are consistent with a provider that dribbles events\n+slowly enough to defeat the idle reset.\n+\n+### RC3 — Compaction-stuck breaker fires late\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:218-241` increments a counter on each no-op\n+compaction event and trips at `stuckThreshold = maxConsecutiveCompactionFailures`\n+(50 by default). On observed `efbf91c5` it took **2h 8m** to trip. If one\n+iteration takes 3 minutes to produce a no-op compaction event, 50 events =\n+~150 min — matches the observed tail.\n+\n+**Impact:** on its own, the breaker is too slow to be a useful hang-guard. It\n+should be replaced or complemented by a wall-clock-bounded variant.\n+\n+### RC4 — Provider HTTP requests likely lack a per-request deadline\n+\n+`RunAgent` passes the internal `context.Background()`-derived ctx into\n+`agentlib.Run(ctx, req)`. The agent library's providers (`openai.Client`,\n+anthropic client) do not appear to install a per-request deadline themselves\n+(grepping the v0.3.14 module cache shows short timeouts on discovery/probe\n+only; the main call path has no WithTimeout). A stalled TCP socket after\n+headers are received but before the response body arrives can hang a streaming\n+read indefinitely.\n+\n+**Impact:** consistent with \"alive but mid-LLM-call with no client-side\n+deadline\" — hypothesis (2) in the bead description. Cannot be fully confirmed\n+from source alone; needs runtime `go tool pprof http://localhost:<port>/debug/pprof/goroutine`\n+against a live hung worker.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — BashTool subprocess hang (hypothesis 1)\n+\n+Not the root cause. `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14/tool/bash.go:15`\n+installs a 120s default wall-clock timeout (`context.WithTimeout(ctx, timeout)`)\n+and calls `exec.CommandContext`. Per-call bash hangs self-terminate at 2min.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — Tracker-side claim leak (hypothesis 3)\n+\n+Tracker state is incidental to the hang: the bead's own description notes the\n+hung workers exist as processes (`ps aux | grep claude`). The worker process is\n+alive; the claim is alive because the worker is alive. Fixing the claim alone\n+does not kill the worker. This hypothesis is downstream, not upstream.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — Worktree file-lock (hypothesis 4)\n+\n+No evidence in bundles. Execute-bead worktrees are plain git worktrees at\n+`/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/*`; no process-held file locks would survive across 33–142h\n+without the owning process. If the process is alive, the lock is alive — same\n+as RC3.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 3. Relationship to ddx-b808df39\n+\n+ddx-b808df39 proposes an operator `ddx agent workers stop <id>` command that\n+sends **SIGTERM at the OS level**, then SIGKILL after a grace period. That\n+design **does** work around RC1 (process-level signal bypasses the broken ctx\n+plumbing). So ddx-b808df39 is a *correct mitigation* — but only a mitigation.\n+\n+Without fixing RC1 (and at least RC2), operators will keep needing to invoke\n+`workers stop` manually. The autonomous remediations every other system relies\n+on (cancel ctx, idle timeout, circuit breaker) are each broken today in ways\n+that require explicit code changes, not just a new operator tool.\n+\n+**Recommendation:** Ship ddx-b808df39 first (quick operational relief), then\n+resolve the child beads filed below in order RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 4. Follow-up beads filed\n+\n+Each addresses one root cause with a deterministic acceptance test. Priority\n+and ID are shown; full details available via `ddx bead show <id>`.\n+\n+The IDs below carry an `.execute-bead-wt-…` prefix because `ddx bead create`\n+was invoked from inside the isolated execute-bead worktree — this is a\n+separate DDx bug worth noting but orthogonal to the hang investigation. RC1 is a\n+prerequisite for RC4 being meaningful.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 5. What this investigation could NOT do\n+\n+- Capture live `ps`/`pprof` from a hung worker — none was accessible from the\n+  isolated execute-bead worktree. RC4 in particular needs a live `go tool pprof`\n+  goroutine dump to confirm that the stuck goroutine is in `net/http` streaming\n+  read, not some other path.\n+- Confirm whether the agent library's providers install any timeout that was\n+  missed in grep.
